quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 9 Mixed Review
quiz.subtitle: Unit 9 Quiz 11: Probability & Counting — Challenging Problems (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Unit 9: Probability & Counting — High-End Mixed Review

questions.question: A card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a face card (Jack, Queen, King) OR a Heart?
questions.explanation: Face cards: 12. Hearts: 13. Overlap (face cards that are Hearts): 3. $P = (12 + 13 - 3)/52 = 22/52 = 11/26$.

questions.question: In a class of 40 students, 25 study Math, 20 study Physics, and 12 study both. Given a student studies Math, what is the probability they also study Physics?
questions.explanation: $P(\text{Physics} \mid \text{Math}) = \frac{12/40}{25/40} = \frac{12}{25}$. Of the 25 Math students, 12 also take Physics.

questions.question: A box has 4 red and 5 blue pens. Two pens are drawn without replacement. What is the probability both are red?
Given $P(A) = 0.6$, $P(B) = 0.5$, and $P(A \cap B) = 0.35$. Are A and B independent?
questions.explanation: Check: $P(A) \times P(B) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30$. But $P(A \cap B) = 0.35 \neq 0.30$. Therefore they are dependent.

questions.question: A committee of 3 is chosen from 8 women and 4 men. What is the probability the committee is all women?
A password consists of 2 letters (from 26) followed by 4 digits (0-9). Letters and digits can repeat. How many different passwords are possible?
questions.explanation: $26^2 \times 10^4 = 676 \times 10{,}000 = 6{,}760{,}000$. Multiply the number of choices for each position.

questions.question: A lottery picks 5 numbers from 1 to 50. You pick 5 numbers. What is the probability you match all 5?
A coin is weighted so $P(\text{heads}) = 0.6$. If flipped 5 times, what is the probability of getting at least 3 heads?
A game costs $\$8$ to play. You roll a die and win $\$20$ for a 6, $\$10$ for a 5, and $\$0$ otherwise. What is the expected profit per game?
questions.explanation: Expected winnings = $20(1/6) + 10(1/6) + 0(4/6) = \$5$. Profit = $5 - 8 = -\$3$. The house has the advantage.

questions.question: Given $P(A) = 0.5$, $P(B|A) = 0.4$, and $P(B) = 0.4$. Find $P(A \cap B)$ and determine if A and B are independent.
questions.explanation: $P(A \cap B) = P(B|A) \cdot P(A) = 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.20$. $P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.20$ matches, so they are independent.

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered Unit 9: addition and multiplication rules, conditional probability, independence testing, permutations and combinations, counting principles, binomial probability, and expected value.
scoreMessages.70: Strong work! Key areas to review: (1) Addition Rule: subtract the overlap, (2) Conditional probability restricts the sample space, (3) Without replacement = dependent events, (4) For 'at least' binomial problems, sum the individual probabilities.
scoreMessages.50: Good effort on challenging material. Focus on: (1) $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$, (2) Independence test: $P(A) \cdot P(B) = P(A \cap B)$, (3) Binomial formula: $P(k) = C(n,k) p^k q^{n-k}$, (4) Expected value: multiply each outcome by its probability and sum.
scoreMessages.0: Review Unit 9 fundamentals: (1) $P(E) = \frac{\text{favorable}}{\text{total}}$, (2) Addition: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$, (3) Conditional: $P(A|B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, (4) Multiplication: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B|A)$, (5) Binomial: $P(k) = C(n,k) p^k q^{n-k}$, (6) Expected value: $E = \sum (x_i \cdot p_i)$.
